title: What do `startTransition` and `useTransition` actually do under the hood?
description: Explain concurrent transitions and how they keep the UI responsive.

title: What is `useDeferredValue` and how does it differ from debouncing?
description: Compare adaptive deferral with fixed-time debounce/throttle.
answer:
`useDeferredValue` returns a copy of a value that is allowed to *lag behind* during urgent work. React renders the stale value immediately and schedules a re-render with the fresh value at lower priority, so an input stays responsive while an expensive list re-renders off the deferred value.

Unlike **debouncing** ŌĆö which waits a *fixed* amount of time regardless of the device ŌĆö `useDeferredValue` is **adaptive and interruptible**: on a fast device it updates almost immediately; on a slow device it degrades gracefully, resolving as soon as React is idle, with no arbitrary timeout to tune.

```jsx
const deferredQuery = useDeferredValue(query);
const list = useMemo(() => <Results query={deferredQuery} />, [deferredQuery]);
```

Debounce throws away intermediate values on a timer; `useDeferredValue` keeps the UI consistent and lets React decide when to catch up.

title: How does automatic batching work in React 18, and how do you opt out?
description: Explain batching across async boundaries and flushSync.
answer:
React 18 **automatically batches** every state update that happens in the same tick ŌĆö including updates inside promises, `setTimeout`, and native event handlers ŌĆö into a single re-render. (React 17 only batched updates originating inside React event handlers; updates in async callbacks each triggered their own render.)

Batching means multiple `setState` calls produce one commit, avoiding redundant renders and intermediate UI states.

To force a **synchronous, un-batched** commit ŌĆö for example to read updated layout from the DOM immediately after a state change ŌĆö wrap it in `flushSync`:

```jsx
flushSync(() => setExpanded(true));
node.scrollIntoView();   // DOM already reflects the update
```

`flushSync` is an escape hatch with a real performance cost (it forces a synchronous render + commit), so reach for it only when you truly need the DOM updated before the next line runs.

title: What problem does `useSyncExternalStore` solve?
description: Subscribing to external stores safely under concurrent rendering.
answer:
It's the official hook for subscribing a component to an **external (non-React) store** so reads stay consistent under concurrent rendering. You give it `subscribe`, `getSnapshot`, and optionally `getServerSnapshot` for SSR.

Before it existed, reading a mutable external store during render could **tear**: because concurrent React can pause and resume a render, two components in the same commit might read different versions of the store. `useSyncExternalStore` forces a synchronous, consistent snapshot and wires up re-subscription correctly, so all consumers in a commit agree.

```jsx
const value = useSyncExternalStore(store.subscribe, store.getSnapshot);
```

State libraries like Redux and Zustand use it internally so they remain correct in React 18's concurrent model.

title: What is "tearing" in concurrent React?
description: Define visual inconsistency from interrupted renders over mutable state.
answer:
**Tearing** is when a single committed render shows UI reflecting *two different versions* of the same external state ŌĆö some components rendered with the old value, others with the new ŌĆö producing a visually inconsistent screen.

It can happen in concurrent React because a render can be **interrupted and resumed**; if an external mutable store changes between the time the first and last components read it, they disagree. React's own state cannot tear because it is snapshotted per render, but external mutable stores can.

The fix is `useSyncExternalStore`, which guarantees every consumer in a commit reads one consistent snapshot, eliminating the inconsistency.

title: React Server Components vs SSR ŌĆö what is the real difference?
description: Clarify the boundary between server rendering and server-only components.
answer:
**SSR** runs your ordinary client components on the server to produce HTML, then ships their JavaScript to the browser to **hydrate** them ŌĆö the components still execute on the client and count toward the bundle.

**React Server Components** *never* run on the client and ship **zero JS for themselves**. They render on the server into a serialized description that the client merges into the React tree. They can directly use server resources (database, filesystem, secrets) and shrink the bundle, but cannot use state, effects, or browser APIs.

The two compose: RSCs render the static/data parts, and `"use client"` components handle interactivity (and can be SSR'd + hydrated). In short: **SSR is about *where the first HTML is produced*; RSC is about *which components exist only on the server* and never hydrate.**

title: What does the `use()` hook do, and why is it special?
description: Reading promises and context during render with Suspense integration.

title: How do Suspense data-fetching waterfalls happen, and how do you avoid them?
description: Diagnose sequential fetches and apply render-as-you-fetch.
answer:
A **waterfall** is when requests run *sequentially* because each only starts when its component renders (**fetch-on-render**): the parent fetches, renders, the child mounts, the child fetches, and so on. Each round trip is serialized, multiplying latency.

The fix is **render-as-you-fetch**: start every request as early as possible ŌĆö at the route/loader level or in the event that triggers navigation ŌĆö then pass the promises down so independent data loads in **parallel**.

Concretely: hoist fetches out of leaf components, parallelize with `Promise.all`, preload on hover/navigation, and let `<Suspense>` reveal each part as its data arrives instead of blocking on the slowest one.

title: What are Effect Events (`useEffectEvent`) and what problem do they solve?
description: Separating reactive dependencies from non-reactive reads in effects.

title: What causes stale closures in React hooks, and how do you fix them correctly?
description: Root cause of captured values and the right remedies.

title: Why does React 18 StrictMode double-invoke your components and effects?
description: Surfacing impure renders and missing effect cleanup.
answer:
In **development only**, StrictMode intentionally renders components twice and, for each mounted component, runs the effect lifecycle **setup ŌåÆ cleanup ŌåÆ setup**. The goal is to surface bugs from **non-idempotent effects, missing cleanup, and impure renders** before they bite you in concurrent React.

Concurrent React may mount, unmount, and re-mount components or render them multiple times; an effect that subscribes without cleaning up, or a render that mutates external state, will misbehave. The double-invoke makes those defects visible immediately.

It never happens in production. If something breaks *only* under StrictMode, that's a real bug to fix (usually a missing cleanup or a side effect during render), not a React quirk to suppress.

title: Why does updating Context re-render all its consumers, and how do you fix it?
description: Context lacks selectors; strategies to avoid wide re-renders.
answer:
Every component calling `useContext` re-renders whenever the context **value changes by reference**. Context has no built-in selector, so even a consumer that reads a single field re-renders on any value change ŌĆö and if you create a new value object each render, *all* consumers re-render every time.

Fixes, roughly in order of reach:

- **Memoize the value** so its reference is stable when the underlying data hasn't changed.
- **Split** one broad context into several narrow ones (e.g., separate *data* from *dispatch/setters*) so readers of stable setters don't re-render on data changes.
- Use an **external store with selectors** (`useSyncExternalStore`, Zustand, Redux) so components subscribe to *slices* and re-render only when their slice changes.

```jsx
const value = useMemo(() => ({ user, logout }), [user]);
return <AuthContext.Provider value={value}>{children}</AuthContext.Provider>;
```

title: What can and can't an Error Boundary catch?
description: The precise scope of error boundaries and how to cover the gaps.
answer:
An **Error Boundary** catches errors thrown during **rendering**, in **lifecycle methods**, and in **constructors** of the components *below* it, letting you show a fallback UI.

It does **not** catch:

- Errors in **event handlers** (use a normal `try/catch`).
- Errors in **asynchronous** code (`setTimeout`, promises, `async` callbacks).
- Errors during **server-side rendering**.
- Errors thrown in the **boundary component itself**.

Boundaries must be **class components** implementing `getDerivedStateFromError` (to render fallback) and/or `componentDidCatch` (to log). For the uncovered cases, catch the error manually and, if you want a fallback, set error state yourself.

title: How does streaming SSR with selective hydration work?
description: renderToPipeableStream, Suspense streaming, and prioritized hydration.
answer:
`renderToPipeableStream` **streams HTML in chunks** as it's produced, so the browser can paint content before the whole page is ready. Anything wrapped in `<Suspense>` can stream its fallback first and its real content later when the data resolves.

**Selective hydration** then hydrates ready chunks **independently**, and **prioritizes** the regions the user interacts with: if a user clicks an un-hydrated part, React hydrates *that* subtree first, ahead of the others.

The effect is that HTML streaming and JS hydration are **decoupled** ŌĆö slow data in one boundary no longer blocks displaying or hydrating the rest of the page, dramatically improving time-to-interactive on large pages.

title: What causes hydration mismatches, and how do you handle them?
description: Server/client first-render divergence and correct remedies.
answer:
A **hydration mismatch** occurs when the HTML React rendered on the **server** differs from what the **client** produces on its first render pass. Common causes: `Date.now()`/`Math.random()`, reading `window`/`localStorage`, locale or timezone differences, or invalid HTML nesting (e.g., a `
` inside a `

`). React warns and may **discard** the server HTML for the mismatched subtree, hurting performance and sometimes flashing content. Fixes: - Make the first render **deterministic** (don't read client-only values during it). - Gate browser-only output behind a **mounted flag**, rendering it only after the first client render. - Use `suppressHydrationWarning` for intentionally divergent content such as timestamps. ```jsx const [mounted, setMounted] = useState(false); useEffect(() => setMounted(true), []); return {mounted ? new Date().toLocaleTimeString() : null}; ```

title: When and why do you use `forwardRef` with `useImperativeHandle`?
description: Exposing a curated imperative API from a component.

title: How can the `key` prop be used to deliberately reset component state?
description: Remounting via key changes instead of syncing props to state.
answer:
React identifies a component by its **position and `key`**. Changing the `key` makes React treat it as a *different* component: it **unmounts** the old instance (running cleanup) and **mounts a fresh one**, discarding all state and effects.

This is the idiomatic way to **reset** a subtree. For example, give a form a key tied to the entity it edits:

```jsx
<UserForm key={userId} userId={userId} />
```

Each `userId` gets a clean form with no leftover state ŌĆö more reliable than copying props into state inside an effect. The flip side is the classic bug: an **unstable key** (like an array index that reorders) causes *unwanted* remounts and state loss, so keys must be stable and identity-based.

title: When does `React.memo` do nothing ŌĆö or actually hurt performance?
description: The limits and costs of memoizing components.
answer:
`React.memo` skips a re-render only when the new props are **shallowly equal** to the previous ones. It's **useless** when a parent passes fresh object/array/function props each render ŌĆö referential inequality defeats the comparison ŌĆö so you must also stabilize those props with `useMemo`/`useCallback`.

It can **hurt** when:

- The component is cheap to render, so the prop-comparison cost outweighs any savings.
- Props almost always change, adding overhead for no benefit.

It also can't prevent re-renders caused by **context** changes or the component's own **state**. Memoize deliberately and **after profiling** ŌĆö not by default.

title: What underlying mechanism makes the Rules of Hooks mandatory?
description: Why hook call order must be stable across renders.
answer:
React stores a component's hook state as an **ordered list** (a linked list on the component's fiber) and matches each hook *call* to its slot by **call order**, not by name or key. There is no identifier tying a `useState` to its value other than the position in the call sequence.

That's why hooks must be called in the **same order every render** ŌĆö you can't call them conditionally, in loops, or after an early `return`. If the order shifts between renders, hook slots misalign and one hook reads another's state, corrupting the component.

This single invariant is what allows many `useState`/`useEffect` calls to coexist without you having to label each one.

title: Compare render-as-you-fetch, fetch-on-render, and fetch-then-render.
description: Trade-offs of the three data-loading strategies.
answer:
- **Fetch-on-render:** start fetching *inside* the component after it renders (e.g., in `useEffect`). Simplest, but it produces **waterfalls** because children can't fetch until their parents have rendered.
- **Fetch-then-render:** fetch *all* data first and render only once it's ready. No waterfall, but the UI is **blocked** until the slowest request finishes ŌĆö nothing shows in the meantime.
- **Render-as-you-fetch:** start fetching **as early as possible** (route entry, loader, or the event that triggers navigation) and render immediately, letting `<Suspense>` show fallbacks for the parts still loading.

Render-as-you-fetch gives the best UX: requests run in **parallel** and the UI **reveals progressively**. It's the model that Suspense and modern routers/loaders are designed around.

title: How do you avoid re-renders with composition instead of memoization?
description: Using the children prop to isolate frequently-changing state.

title: How do you safely handle async work and race conditions inside effects?
description: Preventing stale updates and leaks from out-of-order async results.

title: Why must React state updates be immutable?
description: How reference equality drives bailouts and concurrent correctness.
answer:
React decides whether to skip or apply work using **reference comparison** (`Object.is`) ŌĆö for the state **bailout**, for `React.memo`/`useMemo`, and for dependency arrays. If you **mutate** an object or array in place, its reference is unchanged, so React may conclude nothing changed and **skip the re-render**, or render inconsistent results.

Concurrent rendering also reads state potentially multiple times and expects each render to see a stable **snapshot**; in-place mutation breaks that assumption.

Producing a **new reference** on every change (spread, `map`, etc.) is what enables correct bailouts, time-slicing, and predictable memoization:

```jsx
setItems(prev => [...prev, newItem]);          // new array reference
setUser(prev => ({ ...prev, name: 'Ada' }));   // new object reference
```

title: How do you keep the UI responsive during an expensive update without a Suspense fallback flash?
description: Using transitions to avoid the content-spinner-content flicker.

title: What are Actions in React 19, and how do they change form handling?
description: Async transitions wired into forms with built-in pending, error, and optimistic state.

title: What does the `useActionState` hook do in React 19?
description: Managing form-action state, result, and pending status in one hook.
answer:
`useActionState` wraps an action function and centralizes its state. It returns `[state, formAction, isPending]`: you pass `formAction` to a `<form action>` (or a button's `formAction`), and the wrapped function receives `(previousState, formData)` and returns the next state.

```jsx
const [state, formAction, isPending] = useActionState(action, initialState);
```

It removes the usual `useState` boilerplate for a submission's **result/error** plus a separate **loading** flag ŌĆö both are derived from the action. (It was called `useFormState` in the canary releases and was renamed; the React 19 version additionally returns `isPending`.)

Because the action runs as a transition, the form stays responsive and supports progressive enhancement.

title: What is `useFormStatus` in React 19, and why does it exist?
description: Reading the parent form's submission status from a child without prop drilling.

title: How does `useOptimistic` enable optimistic UI in React 19?
description: Showing an expected result instantly while an async action is in flight.

title: How did ref handling change in React 19 (ref as a prop, forwardRef, ref cleanup)?
description: Function components receive ref directly; ref callbacks can return cleanup.

title: How does React 19 handle document metadata like `<title>` and `<meta>`?
description: Native hoisting of title/meta/link tags into the document head.

title: What are the resource preloading APIs in React 19 (`preload`, `preinit`, etc.)?
description: Hinting the browser to load resources early from react-dom.

title: What are Server Actions and the `"use server"` directive in React 19?
description: Server functions callable from the client, integrated with forms.

title: What changed with Context in React 19 (`<Context>` as a provider)?
description: Rendering Context directly as a provider and reading it with use().
answer:
React 19 lets you render a context **directly** as its provider ŌĆö `<Context value={...}>` ŌĆö instead of `<Context.Provider value={...}>`. The `.Provider` form still works but is deprecated and will be removed in a future version.

```jsx
const Theme = createContext('light');
// React 19:
<Theme value="dark">{children}</Theme>
```

Pair this with the new `use(Context)` API, which reads context like `useContext` but **can be called conditionally** (inside an `if` or loop). Together they cut boilerplate and make context more flexible, though the underlying re-render-on-value-change behavior is unchanged.
